summary: My mother, Sara Bard Field, was a noted feminist and writer whose activism deeply influenced my views on women's issues. Growing up with her example, I became very aware of the challenges women face balancing professional and family life. Her commitment to feminism shaped much of my own thinking and concerns throughout my career.

I grew up in the San Francisco Bay Area, a place that sparked my fascination with the oriental world from an early age. The diverse communities and cultural richness here, especially the presence of Chinese and Japanese populations, deeply shaped my interests. Despite this, I noticed a lack of Asian art collections on the West Coast, which motivated my later efforts to promote Asian art locally.

As a child in the San Francisco Bay Area, I often visited Chinatown and Chinese shops, which fueled my early fascination with Asian culture and art. These visits helped me develop a deep appreciation for the oriental world that would later define my academic and professional pursuits. Chinatown was a vibrant place that connected me to the cultural heritage I admired.

Studying under Langdon Warner at Harvard University was a pivotal moment in my life that ignited my passion for Asian aesthetics and culture, especially Japanese art. His mentorship helped me develop a scholarly foundation and deepened my commitment to Asian art history. This experience was crucial in shaping my later work in promoting Asian art on the West Coast.

My time at Harvard University was transformative, particularly because I studied Asian art history under Langdon Warner. This academic environment broadened my intellectual horizons and fueled my lifelong passion for Asian culture. Harvard provided me with the scholarly tools and inspiration to pursue my career in Asian art.

When I learned that Avery Brundage was looking for a home for his extensive Asian art collection, I saw an opportunity to promote Asian art in Northern California. I helped conceive the idea of forming a group to negotiate with him, which led to the creation of the Society for Asian Art. Thanks to our efforts, Brundage brought his collection to San Francisco, greatly enriching the local art scene.

I played a key role in founding the Society for Asian Art to help bring Avery Brundage's collection to San Francisco. This organization was instrumental in negotiating with Brundage and establishing a permanent home for Asian art on the West Coast. The Society became a vital institution for promoting and preserving Asian art in Northern California.

San Francisco was central to my life and work, from my childhood visits to Chinatown to my efforts to establish a home for Asian art here. The city’s diverse cultural landscape inspired me, and it was where the Society for Asian Art successfully brought Avery Brundage's collection. San Francisco became a hub for Asian art appreciation thanks in part to these efforts.

I taught Asian art history at Mills College for twenty years, which was both rewarding and challenging. As a woman in a women's college, I often faced hostility from the art department, but I remained deeply committed to my students and my work. Mills College was an important place where I could share my passion for Asian art despite the difficulties.

Traveling to Indonesia during times of political turmoil was one of the most eye-opening experiences of my life. I was determined to be an informed traveler, learning everything I could about the monuments and culture there. These journeys fueled my lifelong passion for exploring new intellectual horizons related to Asian art and culture.
